summary: I was raised in Mount Sterling, Kentucky, a small town that has remained much like it was when my grandparents grew up. After serving 20 years in the Marines and traveling the world, I chose to return here with my family because I wanted them to experience the same small-town life I had. Mount Sterling is a beautiful, historic community where I now serve as mayor, and it holds a special place in my heart, especially during Court Day when the town feels alive with tradition.

I proudly serve as the mayor of a city within the Commonwealth of Kentucky, which I often call the finest city in the state. Being part of this community means a lot to me, especially since I was raised here and have deep family roots in the area. Kentucky's small-town values and traditions, like Court Day, are something I cherish and work to preserve as a local leader.

I served 20 years in the United States Marine Corps, which took me all over the world and taught me invaluable skills like patience, adaptability, and leadership. My time in the Marines included deployments to Iraq, Afghanistan, and the Gulf War, and it shaped how I view leadership and community service now as mayor. The Corps gave me a broader perspective that helps me lead my small town with understanding and respect for diverse viewpoints.

My wife is originally from Jonesboro, Georgia, a place that has changed a lot over the years, especially as Atlanta has expanded and swallowed it up. She's from there, and I often think about how different her hometown is compared to Mount Sterling, which has stayed much the same. Jonesboro holds a special place in her heart, even though many of the people she knew growing up are no longer there.

Atlanta has grown so much that it has swallowed up places like Jonesboro, where my wife grew up. This rapid change contrasts with the stability of Mount Sterling, where I was raised and now live. It's interesting to see how a big city like Atlanta can transform communities, making them unrecognizable to those who once called them home.

During my 20 years in the Marines, I was stationed at Camp Lejeune, North Carolina, among other places. It was one of several duty stations that gave me the chance to serve and grow professionally. My time there was part of a broader career that took me to 27 countries worldwide.

I spent a year stationed in Washington, D.C. during my Marine Corps career. That experience was unique compared to other postings because of the political and administrative environment. It added a different dimension to my service and broadened my understanding of government operations.

Fort Leonard Wood in Missouri was another key duty station during my time in the Marines. Each station, including this one, contributed to my growth and prepared me for the diverse challenges I faced in my military career. These experiences have helped shape my leadership style today as mayor.

Camp Pendleton, California, was one of the places I was stationed while serving in the Marines. It was part of a series of duty stations that took me across the country and the world. Serving there helped me gain valuable experience that I now draw upon in my role as mayor of Mount Sterling.

I was stationed at Kaneohe Bay in Hawaii during my Marine Corps service. Being in such a unique and beautiful location was a memorable part of my military career. It also gave me exposure to different cultures and environments, enriching my life experience.

One of my overseas duty stations was Okinawa, Japan, which was an important part of my Marine Corps career. Serving there allowed me to experience a different culture and work alongside service members from various backgrounds. This global perspective has influenced how I approach leadership back home in Mount Sterling.

I served two tours in the Iraq War during my time in the Marines. Those deployments were challenging and life-changing, and they gave me a deep appreciation for the safety and peace we have back home in Mount Sterling. The experience taught me resilience and the importance of community support.

I was deployed once to Afghanistan during my Marine Corps career. That experience, along with my other deployments, shaped my worldview and leadership skills. It also made me value the small-town life I returned to as mayor, where people come together and support one another.

I was part of the first Gulf War, including Desert Shield and Desert Storm, early in my Marine Corps career. Being involved in that conflict was a formative experience that gave me a strong sense of duty and perspective on global affairs. It also reinforced my appreciation for the peace and community I enjoy now in Mount Sterling.

My grandfather worked as a termite man for Central Kentucky Termite, a small independent business back in the day. He was a hardworking man who probably influenced many local elections behind the scenes. Growing up, I learned a strong work ethic from him and the value of dedication to your community.

Gary Williamson was the mayor before me and actually encouraged me to run for city council. He saw potential in me and helped set me on the path to local government. When he retired, I ran for mayor and was fortunate to succeed him, continuing to serve the community he cared about.

Curt Steger was my opponent in the mayoral election when I first ran for office. He is a very good man, and our campaign was civil and respectful, exactly how small-town elections should be. After the election, we shook hands and moved forward, showing the true spirit of community leadership.

Court Day is a special tradition here in Mount Sterling that I have experienced since childhood. It marks a time when the town comes alive with a unique feeling—chilly air, falling leaves, and a sense of anticipation. Even after years away in the Marines, coming back for Court Day reconnects me with old friends and the community spirit that defines this town.

While not directly mentioned in my personal story, The Wizard of Oz came up as a cultural reference when talking about my wife's hometown of Jonesboro, Georgia, which is linked to the South and stories like Gone With the Wind. It’s part of the broader cultural backdrop that contrasts with my life in Mount Sterling.
